coredata.dc:title: New insights on the role of ROS in the mechanisms of sonoporation-mediated gene delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Escoffre, Jean-Michel
Campomanes, Pablo
Tarek, Mounir
Bouakaz, Ayache
coredata.dc:description:
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are hypothesized to play a role in the sonoporation mechanisms. Nevertheless, the acoustical phenomenon behind the ROS production as well as the exact mechanisms of ROS action involved in the increased cell membrane permeability are still not fully understood. Therefore, we investigated the key processes occurring at the molecular level in and around microbubbles subjected to ultrasound using computational chemistry methods. To confirm the molecular simulation predictions, we measured the ROS production by exposing SonoVue® microbubbles (MBs) to ultrasound using biological assays. To investigate the role of ROS in cell membrane permeabilization, cells were subjected to ultrasound in presence of MBs and plasmid encoding reporter gene, and the transfection level was assessed using flow cytometry. The molecular simulations showed that under sonoporation conditions, ROS can form inside the MBs. These radicals could easily diffuse through the MB shell toward the surrounding aqueous phase and participate in the permeabilization of nearby cell membranes. Experimental data confirmed that MBs favor spontaneous formation of a host of free radicals where HO
was the main ROS species after US exposure. The presence of ROS scavengers/inhibitors during the sonoporation process decreased both the production of ROS and the subsequent transfection level without significant loss of cell viability. In conclusion, the exposure of MBs to ultrasound might be the origin of chemical effects, which play a role in the cell membrane permeabilization and in the in vitro gene delivery when generated in its proximity.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bubble expansion and water evaporation toward the gas phase
Generation of radicals from water molecules at high temperature – Chain reaction cycle and sustained generation of more radicals
Radicals migration toward the outer water phase
Radicals migration toward bilayer core and peroxidation
Effects of ROS production on acoustically-mediated pDNA delivery

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Permeability of lipid-coated microbubbles during expansion cycles. Representative configurations observed during our classical MD simulations. DLPC lipids are at the interface, with their polar head groups oriented towards water (O atoms represented in red) and their hydrophobic tails (C atoms in cyan) pointing towards the microbubble interior. H atoms (white) and counterions (blue and brown) are also displayed. The permeability of the lipid monolayer was investigated at various non-zero surface tension values to model the expansion/contraction of the microbubbles. Our simulations predict that, under the exposure conditions commonly used in ultrasound applications, the original structure (A) of the monolayer covering the surface of the microbubbles can be altered during each oscillation cycle because of (B) the formation of very low lipid density areas that favor the penetration of water molecules from the solvent toward the microbubble interior.

ROS generation inside microbubbles during ultrasound application. Left: Initial configurations of the model systems used to investigate the formation of radical species inside microbubbles filled with (A) an inert gas (vacuum atmosphere) and (B) with air. The water evaporated towards the interior of the microbubble during the expansion/contraction cycles was simulated by explicitly including solvent molecules in the periodically replicated orthogonal simulation boxes, which are outlined in blue. Right: Spontaneous reactions taking place at the specified temperature and pressure conditions.

Radicals migration towards the water phase. The migration of the radicals generated inside the lipid-coated microbubbles towards the aqueous phase was investigated using the model system displayed in (A). In this model, the hydrophobic tails of the DLPC lipids coating the microbubble are oriented towards the microbubble interior while their hydrophilic head groups are exposed to water. The atoms of the lipid polar heads (N in blue, P in green, and O in red) as well as those of the lipid tails (C in gray and H in white) are highlighted using van der Waals (vdw) spheres of various radii, whereas water molecules are displayed in licorice representation. Na+ and Cl− counterions are also shown as small blue and brown vdw spheres, respectively. HO was used as a prototypical radical. Various surface tension conditions (R/R0 ratios) were employed to simulate the expansion/contraction cycles. The potential of mean force profiles (B) for the translocation of HO through the lipid monolayer covering the surface of the microbubbles are consistent with the existence of very low free energy barriers for this event at all the relatively small microbubble expansion radii, R, considered.

Radicals diffusion in water. Free energy profile corresponding to the H-transfer between H2O and HO and estimated using metadynamics simulations. The difference of distances between the bonds that are formed and broken during the H-transfer event was selected as reaction coordinate (RC). The free energy barrier to go from the reactants well (negative values of RC) to that of the products (positive values of RC) is of ~6.5 kcal/mol.

Radicals diffusion across phospholipid bilayer toward peroxidation sites. (A) A snapshot of the MD simulation of the model system employed to investigate the average distribution HO at a given concentration near a prototypical (DLPC) lipid bilayer. N (blue), P (green) and CO (pink) groups of lipid polar heads are displayed. HO Radical species (van der Waals representation, red and white), and the lipid peroxidation sites (bis-allylic (H11) atoms; black dots) are also shown. (B) Molecular structure of a DLPC phospholipid, same color code as in A. (C) Number density distributions of HO and DLPC main atoms along the normal membrane with respect to the center of the lipid bilayer (z = 0), calculated from the equilibrated MD simulation. The number density distributions of lipid phosphate (P), carbonyl (CO) groups, bis-allylic H atoms (H11), and water molecules are displayed. Note that the HO radicals though confined to the lipid head group area, can reach the H11 atoms (their distribution overlap) and therefore proceeds to peroxidation of the lipid.
Schematic diagram of the experimental system.

Acoustically-mediated ROS production. SonoVue® microbubbles (8 × 106 MBs/mL or 8 × 107 MBs/mL) were exposed to ultrasound (100 or 400 kPa) for 30 s. ROS production (A) was immediately assessed after ultrasound application by spectrofluorimetry using Fluoro hROS detection assay. The effects of antioxidant agents, including Tempol (B) and Ascorbic acid (C), on the ROS production under optimal ultrasound conditions (400 kPa, 30 s; 8 × 107 MBs/mL) for ROS generation were monitored by spectrofluorimetry using OxiSelect™ in vitro ROS assay. Data were presented as mean ± SEM from five independent experiments. Statistical analysis was executed using the non-parametric Mann-Whitney U test. Significance was defined as p < 0.05 (NS, non-significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001).

Effects of ROS on acoustically-mediated pDNA delivery. U-87 MG cells were incubated with TurboGFP-encoding pDNA and SonoVue® microbubbles (8 × 106 MBs/mL or 8 × 107 MBs/mL), then they were exposed to ultrasound (100 or 400 kPa) for 30 s. Transfection level (A, B) was monitored by flow cytometry 48 h later. The cell viability (C, D) was assessed by MTT assay 48 later. The effects of 2 mM antioxidant agents, i.e., Tempol and Ascorbic acid, on the transfection level (C) and on cell viability (D) under optimal ultrasound conditions (400 kPa, 30 s; 8 × 107 MBs/mL) for in vitro pDNA delivery were also determined by flow cytometry and MTT assay. Data were presented as mean ± SEM from five independent experiments. Statistical analysis was executed using the non-parametric Mann-Whitney U test. Significance was defined as p < 0.05 (NS, non-significant and ***p < 0.001).

head.ce:title: New insights on the role of ROS in the mechanisms of sonoporation-mediated gene delivery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are hypothesized to play a role in the sonoporation mechanisms. Nevertheless, the acoustical phenomenon behind the ROS production as well as the exact mechanisms of ROS action involved in the increased cell membrane permeability are still not fully understood. Therefore, we investigated the key processes occurring at the molecular level in and around microbubbles subjected to ultrasound using computational chemistry methods. To confirm the molecular simulation predictions, we measured the ROS production by exposing SonoVue® microbubbles (MBs) to ultrasound using biological assays. To investigate the role of ROS in cell membrane permeabilization, cells were subjected to ultrasound in presence of MBs and plasmid encoding reporter gene, and the transfection level was assessed using flow cytometry. The molecular simulations showed that under sonoporation conditions, ROS can form inside the MBs. These radicals could easily diffuse through the MB shell toward the surrounding aqueous phase and participate in the permeabilization of nearby cell membranes. Experimental data confirmed that MBs favor spontaneous formation of a host of free radicals where HO
was the main ROS species after US exposure. The presence of ROS scavengers/inhibitors during the sonoporation process decreased both the production of ROS and the subsequent transfection level without significant loss of cell viability. In conclusion, the exposure of MBs to ultrasound might be the origin of chemical effects, which play a role in the cell membrane permeabilization and in the in vitro gene delivery when generated in its proximity.

ce:section.ce:para: The combination of high frequency ultrasound (1–10 MHz) and ultrasound contrast agents (i.e., consisting of gas microbubbles) was introduced as a promising method for improving the therapeutic efficacy of drug by increasing their extravasation and local delivery, while minimizing side effects to healthy tissues [1]. In recent years, research in the field of microbubble-assisted ultrasound (also known as sonoporation or sonopermeabilization) aimed at delivering therapeutic molecules including nucleic acids, anti-cancer drugs, peptides and antibodies in vitro and in vivo has grown rapidly [2–4].

This method involves ultrasound-induced oscillations of gas-filled microbubbles (MBs) in the vicinity of the biological barriers (e.g., plasma membrane, endothelial barrier, blood–brain barrier) in order to transiently increase their permeability and enhance the local delivery of therapeutic molecules across these barriers in the targeted region [5]. To this effect, MBs are mixed with cells in vitro or administered in vivo intravascularly or directly into the target tissue. MB behavior in an ultrasound field has been broadly investigated, which resulted in better understanding and subsequent control of the induced bio-effects that can be exploited for drug delivery [6]. The response of MBs to ultrasound waves depends on the acoustic parameters (i.e., center frequency, acoustic pressure, duty cycle, total exposure time). Briefly, MBs stably oscillate over time upon exposure to a low mechanical index (MI < 0.2); this process is called stable cavitation. These oscillations generate fluid flows surrounding the MBs, known as acoustic microstreaming. In close contact with cells, microstreaming leads to shear stress on the cell membrane or endothelial barriers resulting in an enhancement of their native permeability to therapeutic molecules [7,8]. At higher mechanical indices (MI > 0.2), MBs oscillate vigorously, leading to their violent collapse and destruction; this process is termed inertial cavitation. This phenomenon might be accompanied by production of shock waves, which results in greater shear stress on the cells or endothelial barriers in close proximity, leading to permeabilization of these barriers [9,10]. The ultrasound-induced collapse of the MB may also cause the formation of high velocity jets that puncture biological barriers and thereby entail a greater permeability [11,12]. Both cavitation regimes are exploited to transiently increase the native permeability of biological barriers (e.g., plasma membrane, endothelial barrier, blood–brain barrier) through the generation of membrane pores and/or the stimulation of endocytosis, transcellular and paracellular pathways, thus enhancing the extravasation and the intracellular uptake of therapeutic molecules [13].

Although most of the investigations suggested the major role of mechanical forces (e.g., acoustic microstreaming, shock waves, microjets) in the transient permeabilization of biological barriers, it seems however that other phenomena, including the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), could also play a key role in the sonoporation mechanisms [14–20]. Indeed, Juffermans et al. investigated whether hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is involved in the transient permeabilization of rat H9c2 cardiomyoblasts in vitro after ultrasound exposure at low diagnostic power (1.8 MHz, MI 0.1 or 0.5, during 10 s), in the presence of stable oscillating SonoVue® microbubbles, by measuring the generation of H2O2 and intracellular calcium influx (i.e., assessment of membrane permeabilization) [15]. They reported that MB-assisted ultrasound caused a significant increase in intracellular H2O2 level at both MI 0.1 and 0.5. Furthermore, they reported an increase in intracellular calcium levels at both MI in the presence of MBs, which was not detected in the absence of extracellular calcium. In addition, this calcium influx was fully inhibited at MI 0.1 and partially reduced at MI 0.5 in the presence of catalase (i.e., H2O2 scavenger). The authors hypothesized that microbubbles exposed to low MI ultrasound (i.e., no violent collapse of the microbubbles) induced the generation of H2O2 , which increased the native permeability of cell membranes to calcium ions through the formation of transient membrane nanopores [15,21]. In agreement with previous studies [22,23], Juffermans et al. described that acoustically-mediated calcium influx led to activation of calcium-dependent potassium channels, known as BKCa channels, and to a subsequent local hyperpolarization of the H9c2 cell membrane [14]. This local hyperpolarization of the cell membrane may facilitate the uptake of macromolecules through endocytosis and macropinocytosis [21]. In agreement with their in vitro studies carried out on the rat H9c2 cardiomyoblasts, Juffermans et al. demonstrated that MB-assisted ultrasound increased membrane permeability of endothelial cells to calcium ions, with an major role of H2O2 [16]. Further changes in ROS homeostasis involved an increase in intracellular H2O2 levels and protein nitrosylation as well as a decrease in total endogenous glutathione level. Furthermore, MB-assisted ultrasound significantly disturbed endothelial monolayer integrity through F-actin cytoskeletal rearrangement. These disrupted intercellular interactions were restored within 30 min, without affecting the cell viability [16]. Despite the experimental evidences, the role of ROS in the acoustically-mediated membrane permeabilization is still subject to debate. The generation of ROS and their roles in the sonoporation process has been only investigated from a biological point of view. Indeed, the consequences of the interaction between MBs and ultrasound in the production of ROS and their bioeffects on cell membranes are not known yet.

The objective of the present study is to elucidate the origins and the role of ROS in the membrane permeabilization using MB-assisted ultrasound. We harnessed the capacities of computational chemistry methods to decipher the key processes occurring at the molecular level in and around MB models subject to ultrasound. In particular, the initial aim of the work is to theoretically investigate the free-radicals generation (formation) and outcome (propagation and interaction with model cell membranes,) when microbubbles are subject to sonication conditions. Today, advances in computational techniques and HPC resources allow one to address, for the first time, these questions using molecular mechanics (MM) and high-level quantum mechanics (QM) methods as well as hybrid QM/MM strategies. We then confront these molecular simulations to our experimental data. Finally, we discuss a putative molecular model(s), which can support the role of ROS in the sonoporation mechanisms.

We used an equilibrated dilinoleoylphosphatidylcholine (DLPC) bilayer containing 64 lipids solvated with 4386 water molecules from a previous study [24] to generate suitable starting configuration for our classical MD studies. This system was employed as a model of cell membranes to investigate the ability of ROS, e.g., HO to penetrate into the hydrophobic core of the lipid bilayer and further peroxidize it. This system was also used to create DLPC monolayers mimicking therefore the envelop of lipid-shelled MBs [25]. To do so, the simulation box was then elongated in the direction normal to the monolayers to generate interfaces (air-vacuum/lipid/water) well suited to investigate the translocation of molecules, e.g. water and ROS species, through the lipid monolayer in and out the MB.

The all-atom CHARMM36 force field was used to describe lipid molecules [26–28] and the TIP3P model [29] was employed for water molecules. We developed CHARMM-consistent force field parameters for modeling HO and OOH classical behavior. Long-range electrostatic interactions were taken into account using the particle mesh Ewald (PME) algorithm [30] with a Fourier grid spacing of 1.2 Å and a real space cutoff of 12 Å. The same cutoff was employed for the treatment of the van der Waals interactions. Bonds involving hydrogen atoms were constrained using the LINCS and SETTLE algorithms [31,32], thus allowing the usage of an integration time step of 2 fs. Constant temperature (310 K) was imposed by coupling the system to a Nosé-Hoover chain thermostat [33,34]. Simulations of the monolayers at zero surface tension, in which the pressure was kept constant at 1 atm using a Parrinello-Rahman barostat [35,36], and at various non-zero surface tension values, by allowing to change the interfacial area while maintaining constant the dimension of the box in the direction normal to the monolayer, were carried out using the GROMACS package [37,38] in order to simulate the process of the expansion/contraction of the MBs.

Once the systems built as described in the previous section reached equilibrium, we estimated the potentials of mean force (PMF) associated with the translocation of HO through the DLPC monolayer. To this end, we positioned a hydroxyl radical inside the vacuum at a distance larger than 5 Å from any lipid, and we then determined the aforementioned PMF using the UFED method [39] as in previous investigations [40,41]. This method combines the utilization of extended variables that evolve at high temperatures, following the ideas of temperature accelerated MD methodologies [42,43], with the addition of an adaptive bias potential term to the Hamiltonian of the system, in the same spirit that metadynamics [44], in order to improve configurational sampling. The projection of the distance between the center of mass of the HO molecule and a reference point in bulk water on the direction normal to the monolayer was selected as collective variable. This collective variable was coupled with a harmonic constant of 104 kJ/mol/nm to a meta-variable of mass ms = 109 a.m.u. The extended system was coupled to a bath at a temperature Ts = 1000 K using a generalized Gaussian moment thermostat (GGMT) [45]. Gaussian-shaped potential hills, with height of 1.0 kJ/mol and width of 0.005 nm, were deposited every 5000 MD steps. All UFED calculations were carried out using a modified version of the PLUMED 1.3 plugin [46] under canonical (NVT) ensemble (T = 310 K).

Both the formation of radical species inside MBs exposed to ultrasound conditions and the HO diffusion in water via sequential H-transfer events are mechanisms of purely quantum nature. Therefore, they were theoretically investigated using DFT-based Born-Oppenheimer MD simulations as implemented in the QUICKSTEP module [47] of the CP2K package (freely available at www.cp2k.org, released under GPL license). A dual basis set, Gaussian and plane-wave (GPW) formalism, was employed to compute the electronic energy at every MD step. In particular, a triple-ζ valence basis set augmented with two sets of polarization functions (TZV2P) [48] was used in order to get an accurate description of the wave function of the system while an auxiliary plane-wave basis set expanded up to a density cutoff of 400 Ry was utilized to converge the electron density in conjunction with Goedecker-Teter-Hutter (GTH) pseudopotentials [49,50] to describe the core electrons. Exchange and correlation energies were computed within the GGA approximation using the BLYP functional [51,52] in the unrestricted formulation. The starting configurations for the DFT-based MD simulations here presented were obtained from classical simulations carried out in the NPT ensemble in which the CHARMM36 force field was used. Periodically replicated cubic supercells with dimensions selected to mimic the density of the systems at the pressure and temperature of the simulations were initially employed. To investigate the formation of radical species inside a MB filled with an inert gas, we used a simulation box containing 64 water molecules under vacuum atmosphere as model system. The generation of radicals inside an air microbubble was simulated using a box containing 9 water, 11 O2, and 44 N2 molecules. These systems were simulated under various pressure and temperature conditions (NPT ensemble) for about 10 ps and using an integration time step of 0.5 fs. A stochastic velocity rescaling thermostat [53] and an isotropic barostat [54] were employed to control the temperature and the pressure, respective. HO migration in water was investigated using a simulation box filled with 64 water molecules and one hydroxyl radical. In this case, the simulation was run in the NVT ensemble using a stochastic velocity rescaling thermostat [53] to maintain the temperature at 310 K for about 6 ps before starting the below described metadynamics simulations. An integration time step of 0.5 fs was also employed.

The direct formulation of the metadynamics algorithm [44,55] was used to enhance the sampling of the phase space and estimate the free energy profile associated with the H-transfer reaction from water to the hydroxyl radical. This algorithm relies on the addition of a repulsive Gaussian-like potential to the regions of space previously visited, discouraging the system from re-exploring the conformations already sampled during the dynamics and, thereby, accelerating the exploration of other regions. A global collective variable (CV), measuring the difference between the bond that must be formed and the one that must be broken to give rise to the products, was used as reaction coordinate. This selection renders the system from the well of the reactants (negative values of CV) to the one associated with the products (positive values of CV) thus allowing to estimate the potential of mean force and the free energy barrier corresponding to this event. A multiple walkers strategy, which involves the parallel evolution of multiple metadynamics simulations sharing the same bias potential history file, was employed in order to increase computational efficiency [56]. The width and height of the Gaussian-shaped potential hills were set to 0.06 Å and 0.1 kcal/mol, respectively, for every walker. The time deposition interval between two consecutive Gaussians was selected to be 75 fs.

A 4.7-kpb pDNA (1 μg/mL), pmaxFP-Green-C, carrying the TurboGFP gene controlled by the cytomegalovirus promoter was obtained from Amaxa Biosystems GmBH (Cologne, Germany) [57]. SonoVue® MBs (Bracco Research, Geneva, Switzerland) are second-generation clinically approved contrast agent, composed of sulfur hexafluoride gas encapsulated in a thin and flexible monolayer of phospholipids. The physic-chemical properties of SonoVue® MBs are described in [58,59]. SonoVue® MBs (ca. 2 × 108 microbubbles/mL) were prepared according to the manufacturer's instructions. Cell permeable antioxidants, ascorbic acid (e.g., vitamin C; Ref. BP461, Sigma-Aldrich™, St. Louis, MO) and tempol (e.g., 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl; Ref. 176141, Sigma-Aldrich™), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich™ [60]. The concentrations used in the experiments were prepared by dissolving the antioxidant agents in Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline solution (DPBS; Ref. 14190144, ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA). The concentration of ascorbic acid and tempol stock solutions was 50 mM.

Human U-87 MG glioblastoma cells [61,62] (derived from a malignant glioma; Ref. ATCC®HTB-14™, European Collection of Cell Cultures, Salisbury, UK) were grown as a monolayer in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM® High W/GlutaMAX-I; Ref. 61965026, ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal calf serum (FCS; Ref. CVFSVF06, Eurobio, Les Ulis, France). They were routinely subcultured every 4 days and incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator (HERA Cell 150i, ThermoFisher Scientific) with a humidified atmosphere.

Ultrasound waves were generated using a single element transducer (Ref. IBMF014; NDT Systems, Inc., Huntington Beach, CA) with a center frequency of 1 MHz. The transducer had a diameter of 15 mm and was naturally focused at 25 mm. It was driven by an electrical signal generated by an arbitrary waveform generator (Ref. 33220A; Agilent, Santa Clara, CA) and then amplified by a power amplifier (Ref. 125-001; ADECE, Artannes sur Indre, France). The peak negative pressure of the ultrasound wave was assessed in a distinct setup using a calibrated 0.2 mm PVDF needle hydrophone (Ref. 2250; Precision Acoustics Ltd., Dorchester, UK) at the natural focal distance of the transducer.

According to the manufacturer's instructions, the ROS production during the sonoporation and the identification of ROS were assessed using OxiSelect™ in vitro ROS assay (Ref. STA-347; Cell Biolabs Inc., San Diego, CA) and Fluoro hROS detection assay (Ref. FLAPF100; Interchim, Montluçon, France), respectively. The ROS production was immediately measured after ultrasound application using Fluoro hROS detection assay, which is known to be more sensitive to the hydroxyl radical. However, this assay cannot be used to assess the ROS production during the ultrasound exposure because such treatment inactivates the fluorescent probe. It’s the main reason why we use the OxiSelect™ ROS assay in order to detect the ROS generation during ultrasound application.

As previously described [63], the MB suspension (8 × 106 or 8 × 107 SonoVue® microbubbles/mL in 1.5 mL medium) was placed in the polystyrene cuvette (45 mm height, 10 mm inside diameter, 12 mm outside diameter; Ref. 55143; Fisher Scientific SAS, Illkirch, France) with or without antioxidant agents (1 or 2 mM). The center of the cuvette was positioned at the focal distance of the transducer in a deionized water tank at 37 °C (Fig. 1 ). The MB suspension was kept uniform using gentle magnetic stirring (Ref. 250194; ThermoFisher Scientific) during ultrasound application. Subsequently, the microbubble suspension was exposed to 1 MHz sinusoid ultrasound waves for 30 s with a pulse repetition period of 100 μs, 40 cycles per pulse (i.e., optimal acoustic parameters for gene or drug delivery) [64,65]. Two acoustic pressures were used: 100 kPa and 400 kPa peak negative pressures.

After ultrasound application, 200 μL of sample aliquots were immediately placed in 96-well plates and the fluorescence was measured by a SpectraMax® Gemini XPS microplate reader (Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA) according to the manufacturer's instructions.

As previously described [57], U-87 MG cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin-EDTA (Ref. 25200056; ThermoFisher Scientific) and resuspended in OptiMEM® High W/GlutaMAX-I (Ref. 51985042; ThermoFisher Scientific) supplemented with 1% FCS. During the procedure, the cell suspension was maintained at 37 °C in a water bath. Using the same set-up and procedure previously described for in vitro ROS detection, the cell suspension (5 × 105 cells in 1.5 mL medium) was placed in the plastic cuvette. Then, 6.25 or 62.5 μL of SonoVue® MBs (i.e., 8 × 106 or 8 × 107 microbubbles/mL) and 7.1 μL of pDNA (1 μg/μL) with or without antioxidant agents (1 or 2 mM) were added 1 min before ultrasound application. Subsequently, the cell suspension was exposed to the same ultrasound parameters as previously described.

After ultrasound application, 500 μL of cells were cultured in 24-well cell culture plates (Ref. 3524; Corning Life Science, BV, Amsterdam, the Netherlands) and incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator with a humidified atmosphere. Four hours later, the final concentration of FCS was increased to 10% and the 24-well cell culture plates were incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator with a humidified atmosphere for 48 h.

Forty-eight hours later, transfection level (i.e., percentage of GFP-expressing cells) was assessed by flow cytometry. The cells were harvested by trypsinization and resuspended in 1 mL OptiMEM® High W/GlutaMAX-I supplemented with 10% FCS. The cell suspension was centrifuged (i.e., 3 min, 800 g; SIGMA 2-6E, ThermoFisher Scientific) and then resuspended in 300 μL DPBS supplemented with 1% FCS. Fluorescence histograms were recorded using a Gallios flow cytometer (Beckman Coulter Inc., Fullerton, CA) and analyzed using the Kaluza software (v.2.1.1; Beckman Coulter Inc.) supplied by the manufacturer. A minimum of 50 000 events (i.e., 50 000 cells) was analyzed to generate each histogram. The gate was arbitrary set for the detection of green fluorescence intensity.

The cell viability was evaluated using a methylthiazolydiphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) colorimetric assay [61]. Forty-eight hours after in vitro pDNA delivery, the cell medium was replaced with 150 μL of 0.5 mg/mL MTT solution (Ref. 6494; ThermoFisher Scientific), and the cells were incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator with a humidified atmosphere for 1 h. Afterwards, the MTT solution was substituted by 200 μL of pure dimethyl sulfoxide solution (Ref. 276866; Sigma-Aldrich™), and the cells were incubated for 10 min under gentle agitation (i.e., 20 rpm) at room temperature. Using iMark™ microplate reader (Bio-Rad, Marnes-la-Coquette, France), the optical density (OD) was then measured at 570 nm (OD570) to determine the amount of formazan crystal formation. Measurement at 690 nm (OD690) was used as a reference. The cell viability was calculated as:

Data were presented as mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM) from five independent experiments. Statistical analysis was executed using the non-parametric Mann-Whitney U test (GraphPad Prism v6, GraphPad Software, Inc, La Jolla, CA). Significance was defined as p < 0.05 (NS, non-significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bubble expansion and water evaporation toward the gas phase
ce:section.ce:para: When exposed to ultrasound, MBs undergo large oscillations during which a significant increase in the volume of the MB occurs. Consequently, their initial radius (R0) enlarges at each cycle to a value R, that is highly dependent on the original size of the MB, its lipid envelop composition, the dissolved gas content and the applied acoustic parameters [66,67]. The permeability of lipid-shelled MBs during the expansion cycles was examined here using classical MD simulations. We investigated indeed, using computational modeling, the translocation of water molecules from the solvent toward the vacuum slab (Fig. 1) representing the interior of the MBs, through the lipid monolayer covering its surface. We modeled the MB lipid monolayer at three different surface tension values, leading to MB expansions corresponding to R/R0 ratios of 1.00 (zero surface tension), 1.08, and 1.16, although it has been reported that even larger final radii (R) can be reached [66,67]. Note here that the monolayer surface area expended during these simulations by the square of this ratio. At the highest ratio studied here, the expansion of the monolayer leads to the formation of several areas of very low lipid density where water is exposed to vacuum (Fig. 1). These regions allow for spontaneous evaporation of several water molecules during the time lapse of the conducted MD runs (~100 ns). Hence, our results predict that during each oscillation cycle of commonly used ultrasound exposures in biological applications water vapor penetrates into the MB interior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of radicals from water molecules at high temperature – Chain reaction cycle and sustained generation of more radicals
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the potential of these water molecules inside the MBs to generate reactive species when encountering the extreme conditions created inside the MBs during ultrasound application. Various scenarios, at different inner gas pressure, temperature, and gas contents in the MB interior, were considered to see the impact of all these variables on the ultrasound-promoted radical generation.

The simulations show that once OH and H were formed, several reaction channels were triggered spontaneously. In particular, we initially observed quick and nearly barrierless bimolecular processes leading to the formation of new species:
2HO → HOO + H
H + H2O → HO + H2

Then, as the probability of recombination processes is proportional to the concentration of highly reactive radical species inside the microbubble, water starts being regenerated to some extent through the following reactions:
HOO + HO → H2O + O2
H + HO → H2O

Interestingly, our simulations predict that, even if it is not initially present inside the bubble (the case of many commercially available US contrast agents), O2 is rapidly produced after H2O migration from the aqueous phase as a consequence of the ultrasound-induced extreme temperatures created in the interior of the MB. This generated O2 is expected to increase the diversity of radical species that are generated in the microbubble core because of the multiple reaction paths offered by the diradical oxygen. These presumed reaction channels are discussed below together with the impact of using air bubbles on ROS generation.

In a second set of simulations, in order to investigate the formation of radical species inside a MB filled with air, we used a periodic simulation box containing 14% H2O + 86% air (80% N2 + 20% O2) as model system (Fig. 2). A pressure of about 7000 atm has been predicted to be reached in the compression cycles inside air bubbles before their transient collapse [68]. Therefore, to mimic this condition, we performed the simulations at a very high pressure (6768 atm). Our MD simulations allow us confirming the absence of radical formation when inner temperatures are below 6000 K. On the other hand, the thermal energy provided at this temperature is high enough to cause the homolytic rupture of H2O molecules (H2O → HO + H). Upon initiation, diverse reaction channels are possible. As mentioned above, HOO is subsequently formed because of the HO recombination (2HO → HOO + H). In addition, both HO + H can rapidly react with either N2 or O2 giving rise to other highly reactive species such as N2H or O according to the following reactions:
HO + O2 → HOO + O
H + O2 → HO + O
H + N2 → N2H
HO + N2 → N2H + O

Interestingly, once O is produced, O3 can also be generated in the media in the presence of O2:
O + O2 → O3

Therefore, the carried out DFT-based MD simulations agree with a mechanism according to which the high temperatures induced inside microbubbles exposed to ultrasounds create the appropriate conditions to initiate ROS generation via the homolytic breakage of water molecules. Furthermore, as the rate of formation of radical species as well as that of their recombination depend on the probability of encounters between the species inside the bubble, high pressures can accelerate the aforementioned events.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radicals migration toward the outer water phase
ce:section.ce:para: To theoretically inspect the ability of the radicals generated inside the lipid-shelled MBs to migrate towards the aqueous phase during the expansion/contraction cycles, we performed new classical MD simulations under the same surface tension conditions (R/R0 ratios) employed in the previous section. In this case, we estimated the PMF corresponding to the translocation of HO (as a prototypical and most potent radical) through the microbubble lipid monolayer to the bulk solvent. The results, displayed in Fig. 3 , predict that HO permeation through the DLPC monolayer is a feasible process which requires only a very low free energy barrier of about 4 kJ/mol, already at the relatively small MB expansion radii (R) considered in our simulations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radicals migration toward bilayer core and peroxidation
ce:section.ce:para: We finally explored the potential of hydroxyl radicals to diffuse in water, once outside the MB, and reach peroxidation sites such as the core of bilayer membranes of neighboring cells. To this end, we used a combination of DFT- and force field-based MD simulations. Two consecutive steps of very different nature can be clearly identified in this diffusion mechanism: (i) HO migration in the aqueous phase from the surface of the MB towards the neighborhood of the cellular membrane, and (ii) HO penetration into the membrane bilayer to abstract the weakly bound H-atoms located in the lipid tails.

The diffusion of HO from the bulk solvent towards the vicinity of the cellular membrane via a previously reported mechanism [69], which involves sequential H-transfer events, was investigated using enhanced sampling metadynamics simulations (see the Material and Methods section for further details). The reconstructed free energy profile corresponding to one of these H-transfer events between H2O and HO is shown in Fig. 4 . The estimated barrier is of about 6.5 kcal/mol, which is fairly consistent with a very fast process occurring in a nanosecond time scale. Noticeably, possible quantum tunneling effects were not taken into consideration in our study. As the contribution of these effects would further increase the kinetics of the H-hopping events on the transition rate, the aforementioned time scale must be regarded as an upper limit for the process.

Hydroxyl radicals are known to be very reactive species, with a half-life of the order of 10−9 s, capable of abstracting weakly bound H atoms from polyunsaturated lipids to initiate lipid peroxidation [70]. This indicates that H-abstraction is an extremely fast reaction provided that the reactants come into close proximity. Along the lines of previous simulations showing that HO can penetrate deep in the hydrophobic core of lipids (POPC bilayers) [71], we investigated the ability of this radical HO to penetrate and diffuse across a prototypical membrane bilayer composed by DLPC phospholipids in order to reach the peroxidation sites, namely their bis-allylic H atoms. To this end, we positioned hydroxyl radicals in close proximity to a well equilibrated lipid bilayer [24] and performed extended classical MD simulations (800 ns) to determine the frequency of encounters between HO and the weakly bound bis-allylic H atoms that are present in the unsaturated lipid tails. The results, displayed in Fig. 5 , clearly show that regions below the lipid headgroups are accessible to HO and, therefore, there is certain overlap between the equilibrium probability distributions of the hydroxyl radical and that of the DLPC bis-allylic H atoms (H11). The existence of this overlap confirms that HO as well as other reactive species with similar physicochemical properties can penetrate the bilayer and initiate lipid peroxidation in cell membranes once expelled from the lipid-shelled MBs in which they are generated.

Altogether, our theoretical investigation predicts hence that during the expansion phase of the MB following ultrasound application, water vapor penetrates into the MB inner volume. Under extreme local conditions of gas pressure and temperature, ROS are produced inside the microbubble following various chemical chain reactions. Thereafter, ROS could easily diffuse in the surrounding medium toward the cell plasma membranes where it might participate to their membrane permeabilization.

The theoretical predictions indicate that a cascade of chemical events is produced following the interaction of ultrasound and MBs. These events are characterized by the generation of ROS via the homolytic breakage of water molecules inside the MB, followed then by the diffusion of these radicals in the surrounding medium toward the cell plasma membrane where it might participate to the membrane permeabilization.

The theoretical predictions have been verified through experimental measurements to investigate whether ultrasound and MBs under selected ultrasound conditions induce the production of ROS. A solution of SonoVue® microbubbles was exposed to ultrasound conditions known to lead to in vitro membrane permeabilization and thus to the in vitro gene or drug delivery (See Fig. 6
[64,61]. The ROS production was immediately measured after ultrasound application using Fluoro hROS detection assay, which is described as more sensitive to the hydroxyl radical (OH).

The exposure of a SonoVue® MB solution to an acoustic pressure of 100 kPa produced similar ROS levels (27 ± 2 a.u. vs 23 ± 6 a.u.; p > 0.05; Fig. 7 A) at both MB concentrations (8 × 106 and 8 × 107 MBs/mL). However, applying an acoustic pressure of 400 kPa led to a significant increase in the ROS level production compared with that obtained at 100 kPa (p < 0.05). Indeed, the insonation of MB solution using 8 × 106 MBs/mL concentration at 400 kPa induced a 1.75-fold increase in ROS level in comparison to that generated at 100 kPa (47 ± 1 a.u. vs 27 ± 2 a.u.; p < 0.0001). The increase of MB concentration from 8 × 106 to 8 × 107 MB/mL resulted in a significant increase in the ROS levels (47 ± 1 a.u. vs 96 ± 25 a.u.; p < 0.001).

To confirm the ROS generation, we measured their production in presence of antioxidant agents after applying 400 kPa acoustic pressure. Tempol and ascorbic acid were used as antioxidant agents and SonoVue® solution was used at the highest concentration (8 × 107 MBs/mL). ROS production was measured during ultrasound application using OxiSelect™ in vitro ROS assay, whose fluorescent ROS sensor is not sensitive to ultrasound in comparison to Fluoro hROS detection assay. As depicted in Fig. 7B-C, 1 mM solution of tempol (0.09 ± 0.03 μM; p < 0.001) or ascorbic acid (0.11 ± 0.06 μM; p < 0.01) resulted in a partial but significant inhibition of ROS production in comparison to control condition (0.27 ± 0.04 μM), while 2 mM of these antioxidant agents totally inhibited the production of ROS (p < 0.0001). Unlike published studies [14–20], all together these results provide clear evidence that the interaction of ultrasound at selected settings and lipid MB induces the production of ROS in the surrounding medium confirming thus our theoretical predictions. Indeed, in the previous studies, the ROS production was measured inside the cells during the sonoporation process. There is no clear evidence from these studies where and how the ROS are generated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of ROS production on acoustically-mediated pDNA delivery
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of ROS on the pDNA delivery using microbubble-assisted ultrasound was investigated by transferring TurboGFP-encoding pDNA into U-87 MG cells using this method in the absence of the antioxidant agents. Forty-eight hours after pDNA delivery, the transfection level (i.e., percentage of TurboGFP-expressing cells) was assessed using flow cytometry, while the cell viability was monitored using MTT assay.

Similarly to the control (without ultrasound) conditions, in vitro pDNA delivery at 100 kPa in the absence of the antioxidant agents did not induce a significant increase in transfection level independently of the concentration of MBs (p > 0.05; Fig. 8 A). Using these insonation settings (ultrasound parameters and MB concentrations), the cell viability was not affected compared with that obtained in the control conditions (p > 0.05; Fig. 8B). In agreement with our previous investigations [57,63], the transfection of U-87 MG cells at 400 kPa at a concentration of 8 × 106 MBs/mL led to a transfection level of 17 ± 2% at 48 h (Fig. 8A). This transfection level is associated with a significant decrease in cell viability, but the latter remained below 20% (Fig. 8B). As shown in Fig. 8A, a 10 times greater concentration of MBs resulted in 2-fold increase in transfection level at 400 kPa (32 ± 1%; p < 0.001), with a slight but significant decrease in cell viability compared with the control condition (p < 0.05; Fig. 8B). In agreement with previous studies [72,73], these results indicate that the efficiency of pDNA delivery depends on the acoustic parameters and MB concentrations. One notices that the acoustic pressure (i.e., 400 kPa) and the concentration of MBs (i.e., 8 × 107 MBs/mL), which induced high transfection level, are the same experimental conditions leading to a high ROS production (Fig. 8A). Thereafter, U-87 MG cells were transfected using these experimental parameters in the presence of 2 mM of antioxidant agents. Both agents, i.e., tempol and ascorbic acid, induced a partial but significant decrease in the transfection level (25 ± 1% and 21 ± 2%, respectively) compared with the level for the control condition (32 ± 1%; p < 0.05), without antioxidant agent (Fig. 8C). As reported in the Fig. 8B and D, the exposure of cells to ultrasound at 400 kPa, with (i.e., in absence of ROS) or without antioxidant agent (i.e., in presence of ROS) led to similar decrease in cell viability, thus suggesting that the MB cavitation is the origin of this loss in cell viability. These data suggest therefore that the ROS produced under our experimental conditions play a role in the permeabilization of cell plasma membrane and in the in vitro pDNA delivery using MB-assisted ultrasound. In addition, these results show that the cavitation has higher impact on the membrane permeabilization and pDNA delivery than the ROS [7–13]. This means that transfection is only partly ascribed to the generation of ROS. One notice that it’s the first time that the effects of ROS production on the in vitro pDNA delivery using MB-assisted ultrasound are reported. We hypothesize that the ROS may participate to the permeabilization of cell plasma membrane through the peroxidation of phospholipids [14–20] in addition to the cavitation process. However, the cavitation-associated phenomena (i.e., acoustic microstreaming, shock waves, etc.) should facilitate the intracellular delivery of pDNA by an active transport of pDNA through the membrane without any contribution of ROS. This hypothesis is supported by the evidence that the incubation of cells with ROS and pDNA on its own (i.e., without ultrasound) did not lead to pDNA delivery and expression (data not shown).

In the present study, the acoustically-mediated ROS generation was assessed with U-87 MG cells only, one set of ultrasound parameters (with two acoustic pressures, 100 and 400 kPa) and one type of MBs (SonoVue®), and the use of these settings with our sonoporation device led to a reversible membrane permeabilization and in vitro gene transfection [57,61–64]. In our previous studies, we evaluated the impact of other settings (pulse length, duty cycle, etc) on sonoporation efficiency and showed indeed a strong correlation. In the current study, we selected 2 distinct acoustic pressures as they represent somehow extreme conditions: low or no sonoporation at 100 kPa and highest sonoporation (with lowest cell mortality) at 400 kPa. Indeed, it would be very interesting to correlate the ROS formation with other ultrasound parameters including those that induce cell mortality. We hope we will be able to extend this study in the near future by varying ultrasound parameters and also testing different microbubbles (e.g., different lipid shells) or using targeted microbubbles.

In summary, the present study highlights a positive correlation between the acoustically-mediated production of ROS and the subsequent membrane permeabilization using computational chemistry methods and biochemical techniques. Indeed, the exposure of MBs to ultrasound induces the formation of ROS inside the MBs. These radicals could easily then diffuse through the microbubble shell toward the surrounding aqueous phase and participate to the process of cell membrane permeabilization and gene delivery.
